title: End of module: Algorithms 1 — Searching and Sorting

questions.text: Which condition must the input list satisfy before binary search can be used on it?
choices: It must be sorted
It must contain only unique values
It must have an even number of elements
It must contain only positive numbers
questions.explanation: Binary search discards half the remaining list based on a single comparison with the midpoint. That is only valid if every element on the discarded side is guaranteed to be on the wrong side of the target too — which requires the list to be sorted.

questions.text: The sorted list is $[3, 7, 12, 19, 24, 31, 42, 50]$ (8 elements, indices 0–7). Using binary search, how many comparisons are needed to find the value 42?
questions.explanation: Trace: mid index 3 (value 19), $19 < 42$, discard left → mid index 5 (value 31), $31 < 42$, discard left → mid index 6 (value 42), found. That is 3 comparisons.

questions.text: Using the same list $[3, 7, 12, 19, 24, 31, 42, 50]$, how many comparisons does binary search need to find the value 50?
questions.explanation: Trace: mid index 3 (19), $19<50$ → mid index 5 (31), $31<50$ → mid index 6 (42), $42<50$ → mid index 7 (50), found. That is 4 comparisons — the worst case for this 8-element list.

questions.text: Using linear search, checking from the start of $[3, 7, 12, 19, 24, 31, 42, 50]$, how many comparisons are needed to find the value 24?
questions.explanation: Linear search checks $3, 7, 12, 19$ (no match, 4 comparisons) then $24$ (match, 5th comparison), so 5 comparisons in total.

questions.text: For a sorted list of $n = 8$ elements, what is the maximum number of comparisons binary search will ever need?
questions.explanation: Each comparison halves the remaining region: 8 → 4 → 2 → 1, which takes 3 halvings plus the final comparison, i.e. 4 comparisons — as seen when searching for 50 in $[3, 7, 12, 19, 24, 31, 42, 50]$.

questions.text: Fill in the blank with one word: at each step, binary search compares the target with the middle element and then ____ the size of the remaining search space.
questions.explanation: Binary search discards one half of the remaining region at every step, so the region size halves each time — this is why it needs only about $\log_2 n$ comparisons.

questions.text: For searching a large sorted list, which algorithm needs fewer comparisons in the worst case?
choices: Linear search
Binary search
They always need exactly the same number
It depends only on whether the list length is even or odd
questions.explanation: Binary search's worst case grows logarithmically with the list size, while linear search's worst case grows linearly — binary search needs far fewer comparisons as the list gets large, provided the list is sorted.

questions.text: What is the list after **pass 1** of bubble sort on $[5, 2, 9, 1, 6]$ (ascending order)? Give the five numbers separated by commas, in order (brackets optional).
questions.explanation: Pass 1 compares adjacent pairs left to right: $(5,2)$ swap → $(2,5,9,1,6)$; $(5,9)$ no swap; $(9,1)$ swap → $(2,5,1,9,6)$; $(9,6)$ swap → $(2,5,1,6,9)$. End of pass 1: $[2, 5, 1, 6, 9]$.

questions.text: Continuing from pass 1, what is the list after **pass 2** of bubble sort on $[5, 2, 9, 1, 6]$? Give the five numbers separated by commas, in order (brackets optional).
questions.explanation: Starting from $[2, 5, 1, 6, 9]$ after pass 1, the last element is already fixed, so pass 2 compares the first four: $(2,5)$ no swap; $(5,1)$ swap → $(2,1,5,6,9)$; $(5,6)$ no swap. End of pass 2: $[2, 1, 5, 6, 9]$.

questions.text: How many swaps in total does bubble sort perform to fully sort $[5, 2, 9, 1, 6]$ into ascending order?
questions.explanation: Pass 1 makes 3 swaps ($(5,2)$, $(9,1)$, $(9,6)$), pass 2 makes 1 swap ($(5,1)$), and pass 3 makes 1 swap ($(2,1)$); the final pass makes none and the algorithm stops. Total: $3 + 1 + 1 = 5$ swaps.

questions.text: How does insertion sort build up the sorted list?
choices: By repeatedly comparing and swapping adjacent elements over full passes of the whole list
By growing a sorted prefix at the start of the list, inserting each new element into its correct position within it
By splitting the list in half, sorting each half, and merging the results
By repeatedly finding the smallest remaining element and moving it to the front
questions.explanation: Insertion sort keeps a sorted prefix (initially just the first element) and, for each subsequent element, shifts the larger prefix elements right until it can insert the new element in its correct place — like sorting a hand of playing cards.

questions.text: In insertion sort on $[5, 2, 9, 1, 6]$, what is the list immediately after the value 1 has just been inserted into its correct position in the sorted prefix? Give the five numbers separated by commas, in order (brackets optional).
questions.explanation: After inserting 2 and 9 (which needed no shifting past 2 and 5), the list is $[2, 5, 9, 1, 6]$. Inserting the key 1 shifts $9$, $5$ and $2$ all one place right and places 1 at the front: $[1, 2, 5, 9, 6]$.

questions.text: Which algorithmic technique does merge sort use?
choices: Divide and conquer
Greedy selection
Dynamic programming
Brute-force search
questions.explanation: Merge sort divides the list into halves, recursively conquers (sorts) each half, and combines the results with a merge step — the classic divide-and-conquer pattern.

questions.text: Merging the two already-sorted lists $[3, 8]$ and $[2, 5]$ (as in the merge step of merge sort), what is the resulting merged list? Give the four numbers separated by commas, in order (brackets optional).
questions.explanation: Compare front elements repeatedly: $3$ vs $2$ → take $2$; $3$ vs $5$ → take $3$; $8$ vs $5$ → take $5$; only $8$ remains → copy it across. Result: $[2, 3, 5, 8]$.

questions.text: Which of the following statements about these algorithms are true?
choices: Binary search requires the input list to be sorted
A single bubble sort pass compares every adjacent pair of elements in the unsorted part of the list
Merge sort combines its two sorted halves without ever creating any additional lists
Insertion sort does very little work when the input is already nearly sorted
questions.explanation: Binary search does require sorted input, and a bubble sort pass does compare every adjacent pair in its unsorted region. Insertion sort is efficient on nearly-sorted data because few shifts are needed. However, merge sort's merge step builds a brand-new result list (and recursion creates new sublists for each half), so it does **not** avoid creating additional lists — that statement is false.
